title: Mum Guilt: What It Is, What to Do with It
subtitle: A reading on the structure of mum guilt and how to use it as information rather than punishment
excerpt: A reading on mum guilt: where it comes from, why it is so persistent, and how to distinguish the guilt that is giving useful information from the guilt that is just cultural pressure. Includes a practical framework for working with it.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on research on maternal guilt, intensification ideology, and the psychology of moral emotion.

content.text: A reading on mum guilt. Maternal guilt is one of the most consistently reported experiences of motherhood: the persistent sense of doing too little, being too much, spending too long at work, not spending enough, being too tired, not being enough. It has specific cultural drivers and a clear structure. Understanding it makes it possible to use it rather than simply be used by it.

Research on maternal guilt points to several drivers. One is the modern idea of intensive mothering. This is the belief, strongest in white Western culture, that good mothering needs the full-time, expert-guided attention of the biological mother. The standard is recent. It is not shared across cultures. And it is impossible to meet in any home where the mother works, has other children, or has needs of her own.

A second driver is the comparison frame. The highly curated image of motherhood on social media. The parenting book that implies a right way. The other mother at school who seems to be managing everything better. In every case the comparison is with a selected, partial picture, not the whole truth.

Guilt is a moral emotion and not always useless. The useful version looks like: something has happened that goes against your values, the guilt flags it, and you have the option to do something differently. This guilt has a specific trigger, is proportionate to the event, and has somewhere useful to go.

The other type is chronic, free-floating, and not connected to any specific behaviour that can be changed. It arrives when you go to work, when you don't go to work, when you take time for yourself, when you are impatient, when you are tired, when you simply exist in a version of motherhood that doesn't match the ideal. This guilt has no useful destination. It is cultural pressure wearing the face of a moral emotion.

When guilt arrives, ask: is there a specific thing I did that I can change? If yes, note it and adjust. If no (if the guilt is about who you are, the choices that aren't changing, the conditions that are fixed), the guilt has nothing useful to offer. It is telling you to be a different person in circumstances that aren't changing. That is not information. That is pressure. It can be put down.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on research into maternal guilt. Draws on the idea of intensive mothering. Draws on the way we feel guilt as a moral emotion.
